meta.title: Papal Supremacy and Infallibility | Protesting or Catholic Ep 3
meta.author: Canon Press

questions.question: According to the episode's summary of Vatican I, Roman primacy is described as including which kind of jurisdiction over the entire church?
options: Honorific only, with no legal teeth
Jurisdiction limited to the Latin West
Jurisdiction only when a council is in session

questions.question: The episode claims ecumenical councils have no binding authority unless what condition is met?
options: Every Eastern patriarch ratifies them unanimously
The pope as authoritative head recognizes their decisions
The Roman emperor first promulgates them
They are re-read in every cathedral within one year
questions.answer: The pope as authoritative head recognizes their decisions

questions.question: Papal infallibility, as defined in the episode, was finally dogmatized at which council?
The bull *Unam sanctam* (1302), discussed in the episode, is placed in the context of a power struggle with which French king?
The episode uses which New Testament episode to argue Peter did not exercise universal salvation-jurisdiction over individuals?
options: Paul publicly confronting Peter over table fellowship and extra steps to salvation
questions.answer: Paul publicly confronting Peter over table fellowship and extra steps to salvation

questions.question: J. N. D. Kelly is cited to summarize the Eastern stance on Roman primacy of honor as involving overriding jurisdiction by divine right how?
options: The East fully affirmed divine-right supremacy by the 5th century
The East denied Rome any special honor whatsoever
The East recognized Rome as an infallible oracle on faith and morals
By and large in the negative regarding overriding jurisdiction
questions.answer: By and large in the negative regarding overriding jurisdiction

questions.question: Which Roman bishop (late 2nd century) tried to sever communion with churches in Asia Minor over the date of Easter, according to the episode?
In 256, Cyprian of Carthage and many bishops rejected which Roman bishop’s position on accepting heretical baptisms?
The episode cites Nicaea’s Canon 6 to illustrate what point about Roman and Alexandrian authority?
options: Parallel regional jurisdiction: Rome over its region as Alexandria over Egypt/Libya/Pentapolis
Alexandria was subordinate to Rome in all doctrinal matters
Rome alone could appoint all patriarchs
Constantinople already held second rank after Rome
questions.answer: Parallel regional jurisdiction: Rome over its region as Alexandria over Egypt/Libya/Pentapolis

questions.question: Which council does the episode credit with establishing appellate jurisdiction to Rome for bishops’ trials, even though the council failed ecumenically?
Under Damasus (366–384), the episode says Rome first recorded which distinctive phrase referring to its see?
options: The apostolic see (as a unique reference to Rome)
Servant of the servants of God
questions.answer: The apostolic see (as a unique reference to Rome)

questions.question: Siricius (384–399) is highlighted for introducing what genre of document modeled on imperial decrees?
Chalcedon’s Canon 28 is summarized as tying Roman privileges primarily to what rationale?
options: Papal donation of territories to Eastern emperors
Rome’s status as the old imperial city, with parallel logic for New Rome (Constantinople)
A revelation to Leo the Great on the council floor
questions.answer: Rome’s status as the old imperial city, with parallel logic for New Rome (Constantinople)

questions.question: Which pope does the episode say objected to Chalcedon Canon 28 while invoking Peter’s authority?
The Laurentian schism forgeries are associated with establishing which canonical principle?
options: The pope may be judged by a general council at will
All bishops must move to Rome every decade
Prima sedes a nemine iudicatur (the first see is judged by no one)
Ecumenical councils outrank the papacy by divine right
questions.answer: Prima sedes a nemine iudicatur (the first see is judged by no one)

questions.question: The Pseudo-Isidorian decretals are placed in which century and broader political context?
options: 9th century, under Louis the Pious, expanding clergy and papal power vs. local rulers
questions.answer: 9th century, under Louis the Pious, expanding clergy and papal power vs. local rulers

questions.question: Which famous forged document, included in the Pseudo-Isidorian complex per the episode, claims Constantine gave the pope the Western empire?
According to the scholar summarized in the episode, which claim about councils appears among the Pseudo-Isidorian forgeries?
options: Any village council can depose a pope
Councils must always meet in Jerusalem
Ecumenical councils require approval from Constantinople alone
Only the bishop of Rome can summon a church council; councils without papal approval are invalid
questions.answer: Only the bishop of Rome can summon a church council; councils without papal approval are invalid

questions.question: The episode places rigorous questioning of the forgeries in the 15th century with which figures?
options: John of Torquemada and Nicholas of Cusa
Thomas Aquinas and Albert the Great
questions.answer: John of Torquemada and Nicholas of Cusa

questions.question: Who does the episode say decisively demonstrated the forgeries in the 1600s?
Brian Tierney is used to contrast Paris secular masters with Bonaventure on what point?
options: Whether the Eucharist is a sacrifice
Whether bishops derive authority directly from Christ or as delegates/vicars of the pope
questions.answer: Whether bishops derive authority directly from Christ or as delegates/vicars of the pope

questions.question: Yves Congar is cited (as named in the episode) calling which 13th-century Franciscan leader the principal theoretician of papal monarchy?
The episode associates a theory of papal infallibility (not Bonaventure) with which Franciscan?
The Council of Constance (1414ff) is noted for deposing rival popes and affirming what broader idea in documents like *Haec sancta*?
options: Gallicanism as dogma for all Europe
Immediate papal rule over all monarchs without exception

questions.question: The episode links the decline of Gallicanism’s institutional foothold after which event to 19th-century hyper-papalism?
The speaker’s concluding metaphor for Vatican I’s system, given the historical critique offered, is:
options: A stone fortress unshaken for two millennia
An acorn grown naturally into an oak
